content:
The **universal approximation theorem** emerged from 1980s neural-network theory, answering a question left open after the **perceptron** era: can multilayer networks do more than linear separation?

**George Cybenko** (1989) showed that feedforward networks with one hidden layer and **sigmoid** activations are **dense** in the space of continuous functions on compact subsets of **R^n** — establishing sigmoid MLPs as **universal approximators**.

Around the same time, **Kurt Hornik**, **Maxwell Stinchcombe**, and **Halbert White** (1989) proved closely related **multilayer feedforward networks are universal approximators** results with broader activation classes, including squashing functions.

These results followed earlier intuition from **Kolmogorov–Arnold** representation ideas (1950s–60s) that multivariate functions decompose into sums of univariate functions — later reinterpreted in neural-network terms, though modern UAT proofs follow the Cybenko/Hornik line directly.

**Depth** entered the conversation later: work in the 2000s–2010s (e.g. **Telgarsky**, **Eldan & Shamir**) showed **depth separation** — some functions need exponentially many units in one hidden layer but far fewer in a deeper network. That complements classic UAT: **width suffices in theory**, **depth helps efficiency** for some targets.

**ReLU** networks received universal approximation treatment in subsequent papers, aligning theory with activations used in modern deep learning.

The historical arc: **single-layer limits (XOR)** → **backpropagation for MLPs (1986)** → **UAT existence proofs (1989)** → **depth and ReLU extensions (1990s–2010s)** → **practice focused on generalization, data, and optimization** once expressivity was settled.

timeline.description: Perceptron excitement and Minsky–Papert limits on linear separability highlight need for hidden layers and non-linearities.
Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams popularize backpropagation for training multilayer networks — practical learning separate from later UAT proofs.
Cybenko and Hornik et al. publish universal approximation theorems for sigmoid and broader squashing activations.

timeline.label: 2010s
timeline.description: Depth separation and ReLU universal approximation results refine width-vs-depth understanding beyond single-layer existence.
